extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heat‑bed heater, an internal fan for air mixing, two internal temperature sensors, one external temperature sensor, and a Raspberry Pi that runs a bang‑bang controller. The controller communicates via a RabbitMQ message broker with a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator I/O. The physical twin (PT) operates to reach and maintain a target temperature regardless of the content placed inside the box, assuming uniform air temperature enforced by the fan.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time visualization of the incubator status by listening to controller and driver messages, storing the stream in a time‑series database and displaying it on a dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal models using an automatic calibration routine; (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation via a Kalman filter that fuses measured sensor data with Model B; (4) what‑if simulations through co‑simulation of historical or live PT data to evaluate controller parameter changes; and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that closes the DT‑PT feedback cycle: detecting anomalies, scheduling experiments, re‑configuring the controller, gathering data, re‑calibrating the model, updating the Kalman filter, running simulations, and finally re‑deploying the updated controller. These services support monitoring, prediction, optimisation and automatic re‑configuration of the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a RabbitMQ message broker for synchronization and communication, a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator I/O and enforces a safety protocol, and a controller running on a Raspberry Pi that executes a bang‑bang‑style temperature regulation algorithm. These components form the software stack that enables data acquisition, command dissemination and interaction between the Physical Twin and its Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensors and publishes the measurements to the RabbitMQ communication server; the controller on the Raspberry Pi subscribes to the same broker, receives commands and publishes its state. All data flow is asynchronous but designed for near‑real‑time operation, supporting real‑time visualization and on‑demand calibration. The exact sampling period is not specified, but the architecture relies on periodic sensor acquisition and low‑latency message passing between driver, controller and DT components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin is orchestrated around a RabbitMQ server that acts as the central communication hub for all components. The low‑level driver publishes sensor readings, the controller subscribes to receive commands and publishes its state, and DT services (calibration algorithms, safety monitors, Kalman‑filter based state estimator, anomaly detection, what‑if simulation, and self‑adaptation loop) listen to the same message streams. Calibration algorithms continuously update model parameters, monitors evaluate safety and performance, and, upon detection of anomalies, the DT can trigger re‑calibration, re‑configure the controller, and launch simulation‑based optimisation, closing the loop between physical and cyber realms.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through the RabbitMQ message broker (using AMQP) which relays data between the incubator’s controller, low‑level driver and any DT components. DT components can forward the streamed data to a time‑series database for persistent storage and connect a dashboard for real‑time visualisation of temperature and control status. Thus, the DT interacts with external storage and visualisation tools via standard message‑based interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The incubator DT incorporates two analytical thermal models (Model A and Model B) derived from the energy balance equation Q = c m ΔT. Model A uses two free parameters and relies on four simplifying assumptions (uniform temperature, negligible wall heat storage, constant air heat capacity, and that all electrical power is converted to air heating). Model B relaxes Assumption 4 and introduces two additional free parameters, totalling four, thereby better capturing the heat‑bed’s thermal inertia (Document 2, 4, 6). Sensor data—two internal temperature measurements and one external temperature measurement—are read periodically by the low‑level driver, encapsulated in RabbitMQ messages and stored in a time‑series database for visualization (Document 4, 5). Calibration algorithms run on‑demand (Document 4) adjust the free parameters of the models using the logged sensor data, establishing a model‑data relationship where the calibrated model predicts the internal temperature that drives the Kalman‑filter‑based state estimator and what‑if simulations. The DT therefore combines analytical behavioural models with real‑time data streams to provide simulation, monitoring and optimisation capabilities (Document 1).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the incubator consists of a resistive heatbed that generates heat and a fan that circulates air inside the insulated styrofoam box (Document 2, 3). Both actuators are commanded by a bang‑bang controller running on a Raspberry Pi; the controller receives temperature measurements and issues ON/OFF commands for the heatbed and fan through a RabbitMQ communication server (Document 5). Control parameters include lower‑limit (LL) and upper‑limit (UL) temperature thresholds, a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C) that define the controller’s state‑chart (Document 5). Commands are sent as messages over RabbitMQ, allowing remote control and integration with the DT components that listen to these messages (Document 5). A safe‑protocol embedded in the low‑level driver ensures that actuation commands cannot place the physical twin in an unsafe state (Document 5).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing layer consists of three temperature sensors: two mounted inside the insulated styrofoam box to monitor internal temperature, and one mounted outside to measure ambient room temperature. The low‑level driver periodically reads these sensors and publishes the measurements to the RabbitMQ server, where they are available to the Digital Twin components. No explicit sampling frequency or precision values are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is based on four explicit assumptions: (1) uniform temperature inside the box (enforced by the fan), (2) negligible heat accumulation in the box walls, (3) constant specific heat capacity of the air, and (4) all electrical power is converted to heat in the air (Model A) or, for Model B, the heat‑bed inertia is also modeled. Validation of the models is performed by a calibration step that adjusts model parameters to minimise the discrepancy between simulated temperature and the sensor measurements; the calibration algorithm is described as a calibration process. Uncertainty arises from the simplifying assumptions, sensor measurement errors, and any unmodeled dynamics. No quantitative uncertainty quantification or error‑correction mechanisms are detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature sensors (three sensors, two inside the incubator and one measuring room temperature) are read by the Low‑Level Driver, which periodically samples the sensor values, applies a simple safe‑protocol to keep the device in a safe state, and publishes the raw temperature readings (in °C) as messages to the RabbitMQ message broker. The driver may also compute the average box temperature from the two interior sensors before publishing. Data flow is event‑driven by the driver’s read loop; messages are sent over the AMQP protocol used by RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the digital twin or the controller are sent as messages over RabbitMQ to the low‑level driver, which then actuates the physical twin by turning the heat‑bed on/off, setting heating duration (H), waiting duration (C), and controlling the fan. The low‑level driver validates received commands through its built‑in safe protocol before execution, and the controller also publishes its current control state back to RabbitMQ, providing implicit confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All components of the incubator digital twin (controller, low‑level driver, and DT analytics) communicate through a RabbitMQ message broker. Each component connects as a client to the broker, using RabbitMQ’s messaging facilities to exchange sensor data and control commands. Specific bandwidth, latency, or redundancy requirements are not detailed in the documents, but RabbitMQ’s mature and reliable messaging infrastructure is employed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The controller and the low‑level driver run on a Raspberry Pi that hosts the physical twin, while the RabbitMQ server is deployed on‑premise (same or nearby hardware); the deployment thus relies on on‑site hardware with modest computational and storage needs, and no cloud or edge platform is mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The DT lifecycle starts with a modelling phase where abstract CAD‑type and dynamic differential‑equation models of the incubator are created (Document 1). In the implementation phase the two simulation models (Model A and Model B) are coded and stored in the open‑source repository (Document 3, 5). The integration phase embeds the models, calibration algorithms and monitoring components into the DT architecture (Fig. 1, Document 1) and connects them to the physical twin via the low‑level driver and RabbitMQ communication server (Document 5). During the operational phase the DT continuously acquires sensor data (three temperature sensors) (Document 2) and runs monitors, fault detectors and optimisers; violations trigger human or automated actions. A maintenance/adaptation phase repeatedly executes on‑demand calibration, re‑estimates model parameters and updates the controller configuration, closing the self‑adaptation loop (Document 4). Transitions between phases are managed by calibration updates and by the monitor‑driven feedback, while governance is realized by continuous monitoring for safety and optimality.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The digital‑twin engineering follows a model‑centric methodology. First, the physical dynamics are captured with two analytical models (Model A, Model B) derived from the basic heat equation and a set of four simplifying assumptions (Document 6). The models and the associated simulation code are stored in a GitHub repository that also contains the hardware Bill‑of‑Materials, providing version control and reproducibility (Document 2). Calibration of the free parameters is performed by a calibration algorithm that minimizes the error between model predictions and the temperature time‑series obtained from the three sensors; this calibration routine is invoked automatically after each experiment (Document 1, Document 4). Validation is carried out by operating the incubator under controlled conditions and comparing measured temperature trajectories with simulated outputs; the calibration result is visualised in Figure 13 (Document 4). Quality assurance relies on the calibration step and on monitoring components that check whether the system operates safely and optimally. Evolution of the DT is managed iteratively: models are refined (e.g., adding free parameters or switching from Model A to Model B), the calibration algorithm is re‑run, and the updated models are redeployed, as described in the self‑adaptation loop (Document 4).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides decision support through several automated analytics steps. Real‑time visualization is achieved by capturing sensor messages from the controller and low‑level driver, storing them in a time‑series database and displaying them on an interactive dashboard. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed by a Kalman filter that fuses Model B predictions with measured temperature data to estimate the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and flag deviations from expected behaviour. What‑if simulations can be executed by running co‑simulations that ingest historical or live PT data, enabling optimisation of controller parameters. The self‑adaptation loop uses detected anomalies to schedule experimental runs, automatically recalibrates Model B with the new data, re‑configures the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations, and finally updates the controller settings, thereby closing the loop between insight generation and concrete control actions.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The DT for the incubator embeds safety mechanisms through a low‑level driver that incorporates a safe protocol; this driver periodically reads temperature sensors, validates readings, and ensures that commands sent to the heatbed and fan keep the physical twin within safe temperature bounds. The digital twin also runs monitors that evaluate real‑time data to detect unsafe or sub‑optimal behavior and can trigger corrective actions. However, the provided documents do not specify any cybersecurity protocols, authentication systems, access‑control mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, or formal risk‑assessment procedures.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;

PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].

directly be controlled.

wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and

how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
